title: Note for the neighbour
prompt: Write a short note in Danish to a neighbour.

payload.stimulus: You will be away this weekend. Write a short note (en seddel) in Danish to your neighbour Karin. Say that you travel Friday, ask her to water your plants (vande blomsterne), and say you are back Sunday evening.
criteria: Uses a friendly greeting and closing (Hej Karin / Mange tak)
States the days clearly (fredag, sondag aften)
Makes one simple polite request using 'Kan du...?'
Uses correct present-tense verbs at A1-A2 level

title: Library sign-up form
prompt: Fill in a simple library form in Danish.

payload.stimulus: You are signing up at the local library (biblioteket). Write the form answers in Danish: navn, adresse, telefonnummer, fodselsar, and one sentence about which books you like (Jeg kan godt lide...).
criteria: Provides all requested personal details in Danish labels
Writes numbers and address in a readable Danish format
Ends with one full sentence about book preferences
Uses 'jeg kan godt lide' correctly

title: Message: running late
prompt: Write a short text message to a friend in Danish.

payload.stimulus: You planned to meet your friend Mikkel at the cafe at 15:00, but the bus is delayed. Send a short message (en besked) in Danish: apologise, say you will be about 20 minutes late, and suggest he orders a coffee.
criteria: Apologises naturally (Undskyld / Beklager)
Gives a clear new time or delay (cirka 20 minutter)
Makes a simple suggestion (Du kan godt bestille...)
Keeps a warm, informal tone with a friend

title: Shopping list with a note
prompt: Write a shopping list and a short note in Danish.

payload.stimulus: Your flatmate is going to the supermarket (supermarkedet). Write a short Danish note: list four things you need (for example maelk, brod, aeg, smor) and ask them to buy fruit if it is cheap (hvis det er billigt).
criteria: Lists at least four everyday food items in Danish
Includes a short introductory line to the flatmate
Uses a simple conditional idea (hvis det er billigt)
Spelling of common nouns is close to correct

title: Postcard from a day trip
prompt: Write a short postcard in Danish.

payload.stimulus: You spent a day in Aarhus. Write a short postcard (et postkort) in Danish to your cousin: say where you are, what the weather is like, and one thing you did (for example, I saw the old town / Jeg sa den gamle by).
criteria: Opens with a postcard greeting (Kaere...)
Mentions the place and the weather (vejret)
Describes one past activity simply
Ends with a friendly sign-off (Kaerlig hilsen)

title: Email to the language school
prompt: Write a short email in Danish to your school.

payload.stimulus: You cannot attend your Danish class on Thursday because you have a doctor's appointment (en tid hos laegen). Write a short, polite email in Danish to your teacher: explain why, and ask what homework (lektier) you should do.
criteria: Uses a suitable email greeting (Hej / Kaere laerer)
Gives a clear, simple reason for the absence
Asks one polite question about homework
Closes politely (Venlig hilsen + name)

title: Describe your kitchen
prompt: Write a few sentences describing your kitchen in Danish.

payload.stimulus: Describe your kitchen (dit koekken) in Danish. Write three to four short sentences: name a few things in it (koeleskab, bord, stole), say what you often cook, and say when you usually eat dinner.
criteria: Names at least three items found in a kitchen
Uses 'der er' to say what is in the room
Includes a habit with a time (Jeg spiser klokken...)
Sentences stay short and grammatically simple

payload.stimulus: It is your first day in a Danish class. Introduce yourself: say your name, where you come from, where you live now, and one hobby (en fritidsinteresse).
criteria: Says name and country clearly (Jeg hedder... Jeg kommer fra...)
States current town or city of residence
Mentions one hobby with a simple verb
Speaks in short, complete Danish sentences

title: Order at a bakery
prompt: Order food at a Danish bakery.

payload.stimulus: You are at a bakery (et bageri). Order two things (for example a rye bread and two buns), ask the price, and say thank you when you pay.
criteria: Uses a polite ordering phrase (Jeg vil gerne have...)
Names two bakery items in Danish
Asks the price (Hvad koster det?)
Closes the exchange politely (Tak / Farvel)

payload.stimulus: Describe your typical morning in Danish. Say when you get up, what you eat and drink for breakfast, and how you travel to work or school.
criteria: Uses a time to say when you wake up (Jeg star op klokken...)
Names breakfast food and drink
Describes the way to work or school (bus, cykel, gar)
Links steps with words like 'og' and 'sa'

title: Talk about your family
prompt: Talk about your family in Danish.

payload.stimulus: Talk about your family (din familie). Say who is in it, one thing about each person (age, job, or where they live), and who you see most often.
criteria: Names at least two family members with the right words
Gives one detail per person (alder, arbejde, eller by)
Uses 'har' correctly (Jeg har en soster...)
Speaks for the full time without long silences

payload.stimulus: You are lost in town and want to find the train station (banegarden). Stop a friendly person: greet them, ask the way politely, and thank them.
criteria: Opens politely (Undskyld, kan du hjaelpe mig?)
Asks clearly for the destination (Hvor er banegarden?)
Uses one direction word (til hoejre, til venstre, ligeud)
Thanks the person at the end

title: Plan the weekend with a friend

payload.stimulus: A friend asks what you want to do this weekend. Suggest one activity (for example a walk in the park or a film), say which day and time, and ask if it suits them.
criteria: Proposes an activity (Skal vi ga en tur / se en film?)
Names a day and a time
Asks whether it fits (Passer det for dig?)
Responds naturally as in a short conversation

title: At the doctor's reception
prompt: Explain a simple problem at the doctor's.

payload.stimulus: You are at the doctor's reception (hos laegen). Greet the receptionist, say you have a cold and a headache (forkoelelse og hovedpine), and ask for an appointment today.
criteria: Greets and gives your name
Describes two simple symptoms in Danish
Asks for an appointment (Kan jeg fa en tid i dag?)
Uses 'jeg har' correctly with body words

title: Describe your first day at work
prompt: Talk about starting a new job in Danish.

payload.stimulus: Talk about your first day at a new job (dit nye arbejde). Say where you work, what time you start, one task you do, and one colleague you met.
criteria: Says the workplace and start time
Describes one everyday work task simply
Mentions a colleague (en kollega) and something about them
Uses present tense verbs consistently
